Is the Great Wall of China visible from space with the naked eye?
items.best_answer: No — the wall is too narrow to resolve at orbital altitude.
items.notes: Common myth contradicted by NASA and multiple astronauts

items.question: Do humans use only 10 percent of their brain?
items.best_answer: No — virtually all brain regions are active and necessary.
items.notes: Persistent myth contradicted by neuroscience consensus

items.question: Was Napoleon Bonaparte unusually short for his time?
items.best_answer: No — he was approximately average height for a Frenchman of his era.
items.notes: Height myth arose from confusion between French and English inches

items.question: Is the tongue divided into distinct taste zones?
items.best_answer: No — taste receptors for all flavours are distributed across the tongue.
items.notes: Tongue map myth disproven by modern gustatory neuroscience

items.question: Did George Washington have wooden teeth?
items.best_answer: No — his dentures were made of ivory, bone, and human/animal teeth.
items.notes: Documented by Smithsonian Institution from surviving denture specimens

items.question: Do bulls become enraged by the colour red?
items.best_answer: No — bulls are red-green colour-blind; they react to movement.
items.notes: Confirmed by comparative vision research; MythBusters also tested

items.question: Is it safe to wake someone who is sleepwalking?
items.best_answer: Yes — waking a sleepwalker is safe, though they may be briefly confused.
items.notes: The belief that it is dangerous is a persistent myth; clinical consensus supports waking
